straight_beat.name: Russ Havelock
straight_beat.column:
The D-backs lost again, but not without a fight. Last night’s 9-7 defeat to the Orioles wasn’t the kind of loss that makes you want to throw your hat in the dirt. It was the kind that makes you want to grab a calculator and start questioning the arithmetic of this entire season. Desert math, as I like to call it, has been a recurring theme since 1998, but tonight’s game felt like a textbook case of why we’re still 3.5 games back in the NL West.  

Let’s start with the pitching. Gallen didn’t have his usual “Big Unit” magic, but he didn’t have to. He pitched seven innings, allowed two runs, and struck out eight. That’s a solid line for a starter, but it’s not enough when your bullpen can’t hold a lead. The D-backs’ relievers allowed four runs in the seventh and eighth innings, including a solo homer by the Orioles’ Jackson. It’s not like they’re blowing leads on purpose—no, it’s more like they’re forgetting how to close games. The bullpen’s ERA is creeping toward 4.50, and it’s starting to feel like a self-fulfilling prophecy.  

Then there’s the offense. They scored seven runs, which is more than enough for a win, but it’s not enough to make up for the inconsistencies. The lineup has moments—like the two-run double by Marte in the third, or the solo homer by Carroll in the fifth—but they’re too reliant on streaky performances. The middle of the order, which should be the engine of this team, is

optimist.name: Dolores 'Dolly' Sanderlin
optimist.column:
The D-backs lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. A 7-9 defeat to the Orioles? That’s the kind of game that makes you raise an eyebrow, sip your margarita, and say, “Well, that’s the desert for you.” The sun set over Scottsdale like a bad omen, but I’ll have you know, every sunset here is a promise. A promise that the rotation’s got grit, the bullpen’s got fire, and the dugout’s got a plan.  

Now, I won’t lie—the Orioles played like a team that’s seen a few too many October nights. Their bats were hot, their defense was sharp, and their pitching staff? Let’s just say they’re the kind of team that thrives on making

pessimist.name: Jack Durmire
pessimist.column:
The D-backs lost again. Not because they’re bad, but because they’re the same broken machine they’ve been since 2018. Yesterday’s 9-7 defeat to the Orioles wasn’t just another loss—it was a textbook case of why this team will never be taken seriously. They had seven innings of no-hit ball, a bullpen that looked like it was auditioning for a horror movie, and a manager who still thinks “small-market excuses” are a viable strategy.  

Let’s dissect this like a engineer disassembling a faulty engine. The offense, for once, did its job. Seven runs, a few homers, and a guy named Ketel Rodríguez doing… something. But here’s the rub: the D-backs can’t score runs without the bullpen collapsing.
